jokes.text: legendary 20% tipper... (crowd gasps)
jokes.setup: Building up Vance Connor with impressive credentials like businessman and frequent flyer
jokes.punchline: The crowd gasps at the mundane fact that he tips 20% - treating ordinary decency as legendary
jokes.explanation: Excellent subversion of heroic introduction tropes - the dramatic pause and crowd gasp for something completely ordinary is perfectly structured satire

jokes.text: You're better than us!
jokes.setup: Crowd cheering for successful Vance Connor
jokes.punchline: Barney's pathetic, self-aware declaration of inferiority
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment for Barney - his drunk, self-loathing honesty delivered with such directness is both funny and sad

jokes.text: Sunday's the Lord's day, boy, not God's. Completely different guy.
jokes.setup: Bart questioning if God needs his own special day after Homer mentions Sunday
jokes.punchline: Homer's confident assertion that the Lord and God are completely different entities
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer logic - taking theological confusion to absurd extremes with complete confidence. The casual 'completely different guy' delivery is perfect

jokes.text: A simpler time when the only thing we worried about was total nuclear annihilation.
jokes.setup: Homer nostalgically describing his high school days as simpler times
jokes.punchline: The 'simple' worry being complete human extinction
jokes.explanation: Sharp ironic observation about Cold War era - the juxtaposition of 'simpler' with nuclear annihilation is perfectly structured dark comedy

jokes.text: Please hold your applause forever.
jokes.setup: Introducing Homer as class president candidate
jokes.punchline: Telling the audience to permanently withhold any applause for Homer
jokes.explanation: Brutal economy of language - twisting the polite 'hold your applause' into permanent dismissal with just one word change. Perfect Dondelinger contempt

jokes.text: I spent all night working on that speech, but now I realize the best thing to do is speak from my heart... Um, um, 'Webster's defines--' Um, 'In the words of Ted Nugent--'
jokes.setup: Homer claiming he'll abandon his prepared speech to speak from the heart
jokes.punchline: Immediately falling back on the most clichéd speech openings while panicking
jokes.explanation: Perfect cringe comedy - the immediate contradiction between claiming authenticity and resorting to hackneyed phrases captures Homer's incompetence beautifully

jokes.text: I've never heard a speech so bad. Ten days detention!
jokes.setup: Homer bombing his speech terribly
jokes.punchline: Dondelinger punishing him for the quality of public speaking rather than any rule violation
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of authority abuse - punishing someone for being bad at something rather than breaking rules is absurd but believable for Dondelinger

jokes.text: Anyone who votes for Vance gets a half-day tomorrow. Mm, half-day, eh?
jokes.setup: Dondelinger openly bribing students to vote against Homer
jokes.punchline: Homer considering switching his own vote for the bribe
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer moment - his immediate temptation by the bribe against himself shows his perfect lack of principles and self-interest

jokes.text: Uh, there they are, my favorite mixed-race couple.
jokes.setup: Vance trying to be friendly and inclusive at the reunion
jokes.punchline: Awkwardly pointing out someone's race in a way that's uncomfortable despite good intentions
jokes.explanation: Decent cringe moment showing how even well-meaning people can be awkward about diversity - captures a specific social discomfort

jokes.text: Behind this smile is a bigger smile trying to get out. Oh, oh, here it comes! Oh, yeah.
jokes.setup: Homer asking if Vance is secretly miserable behind his smile
jokes.punchline: Vance demonstrating an even bigger, more genuine smile emerging
jokes.explanation: Good visual comedy concept - the idea of a smile behind a smile is absurd and Vance's genuine enthusiasm undermines Homer's cynicism effectively

jokes.text: No, actually they're like two leather clouds.
jokes.setup: Homer suggesting Vance's fancy shoes must hurt his feet
jokes.punchline: Vance's poetic description of shoe comfort
jokes.explanation: Memorable absurdist imagery - 'leather clouds' is a perfectly ridiculous way to describe comfortable shoes and fits Vance's over-the-top positivity

jokes.text: Like Vance, it is smooth, cool and oh, so sophisticated.
jokes.setup: Moe describing his new Vance Connor-politan cocktail
jokes.punchline: The irony of crude, unsophisticated Moe describing anything as sophisticated
jokes.explanation: Decent character-based irony - Moe trying to be sophisticated while being fundamentally crude is a reliable comedy well

jokes.text: He gave me one of his kidneys. Yeah, me, too.
jokes.setup: Homer asking why everyone likes Vance Connor
jokes.punchline: The casual revelation that Vance has given kidneys to multiple people
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation - starts with simple question about popularity and casually reveals impossible medical generosity. The casual 'yeah, me too' delivery is perfect

jokes.text: About how we give each other haircuts? No, we'll take that secret to our graves.
jokes.setup: Lenny suggesting they tell Homer a secret
jokes.punchline: Carl misunderstanding and revealing a different, embarrassing secret while trying to keep it secret
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection - Carl accidentally reveals one secret while trying to avoid revealing another. The specificity of mutual haircuts is perfectly weird

jokes.text: Okay, but screw this up, and it's Carnegie-Mellon University for the both of you!
jokes.setup: Teenage Carl and Lenny asking for bad recommendations to avoid college
jokes.punchline: Dondelinger threatening them with an excellent university as punishment
jokes.explanation: Clever educational satire - treating prestigious university admission as a threat perfectly captures twisted priorities and academic snobbery

jokes.text: Homer, I had a presidential election stolen from me. But I moved on, and I think you could say everything worked out all right. Isn't that right, Alfred? (speaking in a falsetto, as Alfred Nobel): It sure is, Albert.
jokes.setup: Al Gore trying to comfort Homer about his stolen election
jokes.punchline: Gore talking to himself as Alfred Nobel, suggesting he's gone insane from his loss
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-commentary on the 2000 election with perfect dark twist - Gore's apparent coping mechanism is actually evidence of psychological breakdown

jokes.text: California casual or business attire? California casual. (gulps)
jokes.setup: Homer waking Lenny to dig up ballot box
jokes.punchline: Lenny's concern about appropriate attire for grave digging, followed by his gulp of fear
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Lenny's misplaced priorities about fashion during illegal activity fits his character's scattered concerns

jokes.text: The ballot box?! No, this is the box that tells us where the ballot box is.
jokes.setup: Finding a box buried in the woods
jokes.punchline: It's not the ballot box but a box directing them to the ballot box
jokes.explanation: Solid misdirection with the unnecessary complication of multiple buried boxes.

jokes.text: What will this box reveal? And what will I use this box for afterwards? Mismatched bolts and nuts? Recipes? I may never know.
jokes.setup: Homer about to open the mysterious ballot box
jokes.punchline: Getting distracted by mundane future uses for the container
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - getting completely sidetracked by practical concerns in the middle of dramatic revelation. The specificity of storage options is very Homer

jokes.text: Homer Simpson. Woo-hoo! Vance Connor. D'oh! Please stop doing that. 'D'oh!' or 'woo-hoo!'? Both.
jokes.setup: Homer reacting to each ballot with his signature exclamations
jokes.punchline: Lisa's exasperation and Homer's need for clarification on which reaction to stop
jokes.explanation: Good use of Homer's signature catchphrases in context, with Lisa's straight-man reaction providing the comedy structure

jokes.text: Marge, I still would be married to you, but you would just be hotter.
jokes.setup: Homer explaining how his alternate life would be better
jokes.punchline: His tactless suggestion that Marge would somehow be more attractive
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer foot-in-mouth moment - his attempt to be reassuring backfires completely due to his complete lack of tact

jokes.text: And the taking of a life is murder. And the punishment for murder is-- well, it varies from state to state and by race.
jokes.setup: Homer building up to threaten Dondelinger
jokes.punchline: Casually noting racial disparities in death penalty application
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark comedy - Homer accidentally making a sharp social justice observation while trying to sound threatening. The casual delivery makes it even funnier

jokes.text: And tell him I know what he did last summer... 22 years ago... in the winter!
jokes.setup: Homer referencing the horror movie 'I Know What You Did Last Summer'
jokes.punchline: Completely undermining the reference by getting the timeframe wrong in multiple ways
jokes.explanation: Good pop culture subversion - Homer trying to sound menacing with movie reference but getting confused about the timeline destroys any intimidation factor

jokes.text: No other foursome would have me. My legs gross people out.
jokes.setup: Willie interrupting a golf game
jokes.punchline: His matter-of-fact explanation about his disgusting legs
jokes.explanation: Good Willie moment - his casual acceptance of being disgusting and socially rejected fits his character's resigned self-awareness

jokes.text: Yes! I want him to know that if your life doesn't turn out the way you want there's someone else to blame! Oh, I already knew that, Dad. I'm going to blame you.
jokes.setup: Homer wanting Bart to learn about blaming others for life's problems
jokes.punchline: Bart immediately applying this lesson by blaming Homer
jokes.explanation: Perfect ironic reversal - Homer's lesson backfires immediately as Bart demonstrates he's already learned to blame his father. Great parent-child dynamic

jokes.text: Virgin? What are you, a girl?
jokes.setup: Bart asking for a virgin Tom Collins (non-alcoholic)
jokes.punchline: Homer's outdated masculine reaction to non-alcoholic drinks
jokes.explanation: Weak gender stereotype humor that feels dated - relies on tired masculinity tropes without much cleverness

jokes.text: Hey, what if we got everyone to vote for him, as a joke, and he won? Then we could laugh at him all the way through high school and at every reunion.
jokes.setup: Students discussing Homer as a loser candidate
jokes.punchline: Their cruel long-term plan to mock him for decades
jokes.explanation: Dark revelation about teenage cruelty - the long-term commitment to mockery shows how vicious high school social dynamics can be

jokes.text: You denied me my dream, so I'm angry. But you did it out of kindness, so I'm grateful. I'm filled with respect and contempt for you!
jokes.setup: Homer processing Dondelinger's explanation for rigging the election
jokes.punchline: His emotional confusion leading to contradictory feelings
jokes.explanation: Great Homer moment - his ability to hold completely contradictory emotions simultaneously is perfectly absurd and very true to character

jokes.text: I would steal school chalk for my home chalkboard.
jokes.setup: Dondelinger confessing his past wrongdoings as principal
jokes.punchline: The petty, weird crime of stealing chalk for personal use
jokes.explanation: Wonderful character detail - the specificity of having a home chalkboard and stealing chalk for it reveals Dondelinger's obsessive educational nature

jokes.text: The brown-haired girl gave me a look. The redhead in the park was reading a book. The girl at the airport upgraded my car. Tonight I wonder just where you are.
jokes.setup: Dondelinger saying he'll think about women he could have talked to
jokes.punchline: His pathetic poem about missed romantic opportunities
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Dondelinger's lonely, repressed romantic side through bad poetry about mundane encounters

jokes.text: Yeah, I can actually see your hands. They're not just a blur.
jokes.setup: Marge noting Homer is barely eating
jokes.punchline: Bart's observation about Homer's normally frantic eating speed
jokes.explanation: Good visual comedy concept - the idea that Homer normally eats so fast his hands blur is a perfect exaggeration of his gluttony

jokes.text: Oh, you could know... if you dared. In the kitchen is a man from the old country who works for me. He stirs the sauce-a.
jokes.setup: Homer wishing he could know about his alternate life
jokes.punchline: Luigi's mysterious introduction to his magical sauce-stirrer
jokes.explanation: Good setup for the fantasy sequence - Luigi's dramatic mystical persona mixed with mundane restaurant work creates the right tone

jokes.text: Also, as a vegetarian, I hope there's not meat in that sauce. Any other orders, Mussolini?
jokes.setup: Lisa expressing vegetarian concerns about the sauce
jokes.punchline: Luigi's sarcastic Mussolini reference in response to her demands
jokes.explanation: Decent character interaction - Luigi's frustration with Lisa's demands leads to an appropriately Italian historical insult

jokes.text: Our president's a real loser! Yeah. A loser... like us! He proves you don't have to be popular to have everyone like you!
jokes.setup: Students initially mocking President Homer
jokes.punchline: Their realization that his loser status makes him relatable and inspiring
jokes.explanation: Good ironic reversal - what was meant as mockery becomes genuine inspiration as the students find hope in Homer's ordinariness

jokes.text: Hey, everyone! The stupid fat man is talking to garlic bread!
jokes.setup: Homer asking philosophical questions to garlic bread
jokes.punchline: Other diners mocking his bizarre behavior
jokes.explanation: Basic cringe humor - Homer's public breakdown becomes entertainment for others, but lacks sophistication

jokes.text: 'Color My World,' 'Disco Sucks' and Butthead the Goat.
jokes.setup: Presidential Homer being asked for class song, motto, and mascot
jokes.punchline: His random, inappropriate choices including a goat named Butthead
jokes.explanation: Great Homer decisions - the random mix of sappy song, anti-disco sentiment, and juvenile goat name perfectly captures his scattered taste

jokes.text: They're too big to fail.
jokes.setup: Asked about bailing out the French Club
jokes.punchline: Homer using 2008 financial crisis language in high school context
jokes.explanation: Brilliant anachronistic joke - using modern financial bailout language for trivial high school club funding is perfect satire

jokes.text: Forget it, Marge. A girl like you could never land a Homer Simpson.
jokes.setup: Successful Homer rejecting Marge for popular girl
jokes.punchline: The reversal where Homer is the catch and Marge isn't good enough
jokes.explanation: Perfect ironic reversal of the show's normal dynamic - making Homer the unattainable prize and Marge the unworthy pursuer is brilliant

jokes.text: Nobody's ever asked me to use my head-brain before!
jokes.setup: Homer asking Debbie to read and analyze a book for him
jokes.punchline: Her excitement about using her 'head-brain' suggesting she's not very bright
jokes.explanation: Good character revelation - the redundant 'head-brain' and her excitement about thinking shows why she's attracted to Homer's confidence

jokes.text: I was going to let you get to fifth base. It would've been something you told your grandchildren about.
jokes.setup: Homer leaving Debbie for Marge at prom
jokes.punchline: Debbie's inappropriate oversharing about planned intimacy
jokes.explanation: Decent cringe moment - Debbie's oversharing and the grandfather comment make the rejection even more awkward

jokes.text: Marge, the way I feel about you is as real and lasting as my hairline.
jokes.setup: Homer making romantic declaration to Marge
jokes.punchline: Comparing his love to his hairline (which we know he'll lose)
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dramatic irony - the romantic sentiment is undercut by our knowledge that his hairline won't last, making it both sweet and tragic

jokes.text: I don't know, but in this reality, I am not gay!
jokes.setup: Seeing Homer with their sister
jokes.punchline: One of the twins excited about not being gay in this alternate reality
jokes.explanation: Good meta-joke about alternate realities and the show's continuity - the character's excitement about different sexual orientation is amusingly shallow

jokes.text: 7-G? Let the Lennys and the Carls of the world waste their wretched lives in that testicle-shriveling torture chamber. Son, you'll be working in... Sector 6-F!
jokes.setup: Burns considering where to place successful Homer
jokes.punchline: His dramatic description of 7-G and grand revelation of 6-F as if it's much better
jokes.explanation: Great Burns moment - his theatrical condescension toward regular workers and dramatic revelation of a barely better position is perfect characterization

jokes.text: Oh, I can't complain and I never do!
jokes.setup: Homer asking how Grandpa is doing
jokes.punchline: Grandpa's cheerful claim about never complaining (opposite of his usual character)
jokes.explanation: Perfect character reversal - Grandpa being happy and non-complaining is the opposite of his normal personality, showing how the timeline change affected everyone

jokes.text: Oh, I can't complain and I never do! Thanks for letting me live in your guest house.
jokes.setup: Homer asking how his father is doing
jokes.punchline: Grandpa's cheerful demeanor and gratitude for accommodation
jokes.explanation: Character reversal showing Grandpa as content and grateful instead of his usual complaining.

jokes.text: You used protection. We never had any.
jokes.setup: Homer asking where the kids are
jokes.punchline: The revelation that they prevented having children entirely
jokes.explanation: Dark revelation that cuts to the heart of the episode - their 'better' life eliminated the children, making it actually worse

jokes.text: If you could live in the sauce, don't you think I would live in the sauce? Stupid-o!
jokes.setup: Homer demanding to live in the sauce
jokes.punchline: The stirrer's logical response and casual insult
jokes.explanation: Good deflation of Homer's fantasy with practical logic and casual dismissal.

jokes.text: If you could live in the sauce, don't you think I would live in the sauce? Stupid-o!
jokes.setup: Homer wanting to live in the magical sauce
jokes.punchline: The sauce-stirrer's logical but absurd response about sauce-living
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist logic - the idea that everyone would live in sauce if possible, delivered with perfect working-class exasperation

jokes.text: Are you kidding? We couldn't even convince you that Bruce Wayne is Batman.
jokes.setup: Homer assuming family will try to convince him his life is great
jokes.punchline: Lisa pointing out they can't even convince him of obvious fictional facts
jokes.explanation: Excellent meta-joke about Homer's stupidity - using Batman's secret identity as an example of things they can't convince him of is perfectly absurd

jokes.text: He's too busy socializing at cocktail parties and managing the affairs of the Wayne foundation.
jokes.setup: Homer explaining why Bruce Wayne can't be Batman
jokes.punchline: His logical but completely wrong reasoning about Wayne's schedule
jokes.explanation: Good follow-up showing Homer's flawed but internally consistent logic - he's thought about this and reached the wrong conclusion

jokes.text: Why does he think Alfred's friends with Batman?
jokes.setup: Discussion about Homer's Batman confusion
jokes.punchline: Bart's confusion about how Alfred fits into Homer's theory
jokes.explanation: Decent extension of the Batman joke - Bart trying to understand Homer's warped logic adds another layer of confusion

jokes.text: It's like bringing Richard Nixon to the Watergate. Or Kevin Costner to Waterworld.
jokes.setup: Homer complaining about being brought to the Wall of Fame
jokes.punchline: His comparison mixing serious political scandal with terrible movie
jokes.explanation: Good Homer analogy - treating Waterworld as equivalent to Watergate shows his confused sense of cultural importance

jokes.text: Well, we can't all be Homer Simpson, son.
jokes.setup: Kid asking if he could be elected class president
jokes.punchline: Homer treating his own name as the gold standard of achievement
jokes.explanation: Perfect ego moment for Homer - treating his own name as an inspirational ideal while being completely serious about it
